I asked a simple question in forty two Angular interviews last year.
“Why did your service get created twice?”
Three people answered it correctly.
Three. Out of forty two. And every single candidate had “Advanced Angular” somewhere on their resume.
Here is what that tells me: most of us never actually learned Angular. We learned enough Angular to close tickets. The framework quietly handled the rest, and we called that knowledge.
That works right up until it does not. Until the bug that makes no sense. Until the interview question that ends the call early. Until the production incident where the framework’s magic becomes the framework’s mystery.
These are the five concepts that separate the 10 percent from everyone else. Not trivia. Not framework history. The five ideas that, once they click, make Angular stop feeling like magic and start feeling like a machine you actually control.
Grab your coffee. This is the article I wish someone had handed me five years ago.
Concept 1: Dependency Injection Is a Tree, Not a Box
Most developers think of Angular DI as a magic box: ask for a service, receive a service. That…
